topic: Inflation Measurement, Causes, Effects and Indexation
subtopic: CPI, WPI, GDP deflator, demand and supply drivers, expectations, distribution and policy response

content.issueStory: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level, not every expensive item and not every one-time price jump. Its burden varies because households consume different baskets, hold different assets and bargain for wages with unequal power.
content.coreConcept: Inflation is measured as the rate of change in a price index. CPI captures retail consumer baskets, WPI tracks selected wholesale goods, and the GDP deflator covers domestically produced final output. Demand, costs, supply bottlenecks, expectations and money-finance conditions can interact.
dimensions: Index design: basket, weights, base period, outlet coverage and quality adjustment determine measured inflation.
Demand-pull: aggregate demand can exceed short-run productive capacity.
Cost-push and supply shock: fuel, food, wages, imports or disrupted supply can raise costs and reduce output.
Expectations: persistent expected inflation can affect wages, pricing, contracts and policy credibility.
Distribution: borrowers and lenders, wage earners, pensioners, asset holders and poor households experience different real effects.
Response mix: monetary restraint, fiscal calibration, buffers, trade measures and supply reform fit different causes and horizons.
content.constitutionalLink: The RBI Act monetary-policy framework, MPC process, food-buffer policy and consumer protection connect price stability with livelihood security under Article 21.
content.dataReport: Uploaded source basis: STATIC_01_Economy.pdf inflation and monetary-policy chapters plus PYQ index and inflation-mechanism patterns. Refresh rates, targets, weights, thresholds and year-specific figures from official sources: MoSPI CPI releases, Office of the Economic Adviser WPI releases, RBI MPC statements and latest Economic Survey. Stable mechanisms belong in this note; current values must be dated in an answer.
content.caseStudy: A food-price shock may raise headline CPI and inflation expectations even when core demand is weak, requiring supply action and communication alongside cautious monetary assessment.
argumentsFor: Moderate predictable inflation can ease relative-price adjustment.
argumentsAgainst: Broad tightening cannot directly produce food or fuel.
One national index cannot match every household basket.
content.pyqLink: PYQs test CPI-WPI-deflator differences, inflation effects on creditors and debtors, stagflation and policy tools. Identify basket, stage of pricing and causal shock.

answerFramework.intro: Inflation Measurement, Causes, Effects and Indexation should be introduced through its economic identity or causal mechanism before moving to Indian institutions and policy trade-offs.
body: Define inflation and name the relevant index.
Diagnose demand, cost, supply and expectation channels.
Assess output, distribution and external effects.
Match monetary, fiscal and supply responses to cause and time horizon.
answerFramework.conclusion: A high-scoring Economy answer keeps the accounting correct, traces transmission, identifies winners and losers, and separates stable principles from changing policy values.

mainsExamples: Vegetable price spikes as volatile food inflation.
Currency depreciation raising imported input costs.
Indexed pensions protecting beneficiaries with a lag.
prelimsFacts: Inflation is an index rate of change.
Disinflation means inflation slows but prices may still rise.
Deflation is a sustained fall in the general price level.
Stagflation combines weak activity with high inflation.
Headline CPI includes food and fuel.
Base effects can alter year-on-year inflation without a new current-month shock.

story: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level, not every expensive item and not every one-time price jump. Its burden varies because households consume different baskets, hold different assets and bargain for wages with unequal power.
core_concept: Inflation is measured as the rate of change in a price index. CPI captures retail consumer baskets, WPI tracks selected wholesale goods, and the GDP deflator covers domestically produced final output. Demand, costs, supply bottlenecks, expectations and money-finance conditions can interact.

mindmap_json.center: Inflation Measurement, Causes, Effects and Indexation

branches.label: Mechanism
children: Index design: basket, weights, base period, outlet coverage and quality adjustment determine measured inflation.
Demand-pull: aggregate demand can exceed short-run productive capacity.

branches.label: Transmission
children: Cost-push and supply shock: fuel, food, wages, imports or disrupted supply can raise costs and reduce output.
Expectations: persistent expected inflation can affect wages, pricing, contracts and policy credibility.

branches.label: Policy
children: Distribution: borrowers and lenders, wage earners, pensioners, asset holders and poor households experience different real effects.
Response mix: monetary restraint, fiscal calibration, buffers, trade measures and supply reform fit different causes and horizons.

branches.label: Traps
children: Do not assume WPI measures service inflation comprehensively.
Do not prescribe the same tool for demand-pull and crop-supply shocks.

key_facts: Inflation is an index rate of change.
Disinflation means inflation slows but prices may still rise.
Deflation is a sustained fall in the general price level.
Stagflation combines weak activity with high inflation.
Headline CPI includes food and fuel.
Base effects can alter year-on-year inflation without a new current-month shock.
common_traps: Do not assume WPI measures service inflation comprehensively.
Do not prescribe the same tool for demand-pull and crop-supply shocks.

pyq_refs.question: PYQ pattern: statement and mechanism testing within Inflation Measurement, Causes, Effects and Indexation.
pyq_refs.answer: Resolve each statement through the governing identity, direction of change and institutional role.
pyq_refs.pattern_note: PYQs test CPI-WPI-deflator differences, inflation effects on creditors and debtors, stagflation and policy tools. Identify basket, stage of pricing and causal shock.

pyq_refs.question: PYQ pattern: analyse the Indian policy implications of Inflation Measurement, Causes, Effects and Indexation.
pyq_refs.answer: Use a causal chain, one Indian example, distributional effects and a calibrated reform path.
pyq_refs.pattern_note: Mains rewards mechanism plus policy trade-off, not a list of schemes.
